Comment:
Last spring, someone hacked my daughter's pediatrician's office and used our family's information to open a credit card in her name. It took me four months to straighten out—four months of calls, forms, and stress I didn't need on top of my job and managing her Type 1 diabetes. I'm still getting calls from places asking about accounts we never opened. So when I see this proposal to loosen health IT security standards in the name of innovation, I have to speak up.

I understand that making it easier for apps to access health records sounds convenient. But convenience isn't worth putting my daughter's chronic condition data at risk. Kids with ongoing medical needs are already vulnerable—their information is valuable to scammers and data brokers. The last thing we need is deregulation that makes it easier for that data to be sold or breached.

Here's what I don't understand: if the quality bonus system for Medicare Advantage plans is supposedly such a burden on developers, why does it exist? Because it works. Those bonuses create actual financial incentive to keep patient data secure. Remove that pressure and you're basically telling companies their profit margins matter more than my family's privacy.

I'm a teacher. I'm juggling too much already without lying awake worrying whether my kid's medical records are being passed around to apps I've never heard of. Before you remove any security safeguards, please think about families like mine who've already been burned. Innovation shouldn't mean sacrificing the basics—like keeping our data safe.
synth_objective: oppose the proposed reduction of Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments
synth_personal_hook: Last spring, my daughter's pediatrician's office got hacked and someone used our family's information to open a credit card—it took months to fix and I'm still getting weird calls. Now I'm terrified about all her medical records being passed around to apps I don't even know about, especially since she has that chronic condition that's already hard enough to manage without worrying about who has access to it.
synth_core_arguments: Weakening health IT security standards will put vulnerable patients—especially children with chronic conditions—at greater risk of data breaches and identity theft | Teachers and working parents already struggle to manage their kids' medical care; we don't need the added stress of worrying whether their sensitive health data is being sold to apps or exposed to hackers | Medicare Advantage quality bonuses exist because they incentivize plans to maintain strong privacy and security protections—removing them removes the financial pressure to keep patient data safe
synth_framing: As a parent who's already been burned by medical identity theft, I'm terrified that deregulation will make it easier for my daughter's chronic condition data to be breached or misused.

Abstract: My wife's atrial fibrillation diagnosis last year forced me to confront something I'd been skirting around intellectually for years. When her cardiologist needed a complete picture of her condition, her records were scattered across three different health systems. Comment:
My wife's atrial fibrillation diagnosis last year forced me to confront something I'd been skirting around intellectually for years. When her cardiologist needed a complete picture of her condition, her records were scattered across three different health systems. Six weeks of phone calls and faxes—actual faxes, in 2024—just to get everything in one place. That experience made me realize that abstract arguments about "protecting patient privacy" ring hollow when patients themselves are the ones suffering from fragmented care.

I say this as someone who generally supports reducing unnecessary regulatory burden on health IT developers. The compliance costs are real, and I understand the impulse to strip away rules that don't meaningfully protect patients. But I want to respectfully push back on one piece of this: the notion that Medicare Advantage quality bonuses tied to interoperability reporting and certification compliance are just another layer of unnecessary burden.

They're not. They're the financial mechanism that actually makes interoperability happen at the provider level.

Here's what I mean. When CMS ties MA quality bonuses to USCDI adoption and FHIR implementation, providers fund the infrastructure changes that make data sharing possible. That's not regulatory theater—that's how you get hospitals to actually hire the IT staff and buy the tools needed to meet ONC-ACB certification standards. Remove those incentives, and you remove the funding. Fewer providers adopt the standards. My wife's six-week nightmare becomes the norm, not the exception.

I'm not arguing against streamlining certification criteria or reducing unnecessary burden on developers. But can we be smarter about it? We can reduce burden *and* preserve the quality bonuses that fund the interoperability infrastructure deregulation is supposed to unlock. That's not regulatory bloat—that's making deregulation work for actual patients.
synth_objective: oppose the proposed reduction of Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments
synth_personal_hook: When my wife was diagnosed with atrial fibrillation last year, we had to chase down her medical records from three different systems just to give her cardiologist a complete picture—it took us six weeks and multiple phone calls. I kept thinking about how differently that might have gone if her providers had been required to share her data more readily, and it's made me rethink some of my assumptions about what "protection" really means when it comes to patients trying to get better care.
synth_core_arguments: Medicare Advantage quality bonus reductions would undermine the interoperability investments that enable better coordinated care and patient outcomes | Reducing financial incentives for quality reporting and data sharing contradicts the goal of unleashing innovation in health IT—providers need stable funding to implement interoperability standards like FHIR | My personal experience with fragmented records shows that interoperability requires certification standards and information-blocking protections; weakening these would make my wife's situation worse, not better
synth_framing: A policy analyst who supports deregulation in principle but argues that Medicare Advantage quality bonuses should be preserved because they fund the very interoperability infrastructure that deregulation aims to unlock.

She's 68 and doesn't understand how this technology works. Neither do I, really, but I had to learn fast.

Comment:
My mom almost lost everything last year because of a hacked insulin pump app. Some application she didn't even know she was using shared her medical information with who knows where, and we spent three months dealing with identity theft, frozen bank accounts, and trying to get her information locked down. She's 68 and doesn't understand how this technology works. Neither do I, really, but I had to learn fast.

Now I'm reading that you're considering removing security safeguards from health technology rules, and I'm honestly terrified. Making it easier for apps and companies to share patient data around—that sounds like you're setting up more families to go through what we went through. My mom was lucky we caught it. Not everyone will be.

I don't understand all the technical details about information blocking and what "access" and "use" mean in the regulations, but I know this: seniors and vulnerable people are counting on strong guardrails to protect their most sensitive information. Caregivers like me are already stretched thin trying to protect our loved ones from threats we barely understand.

I also heard that cutting Medicare Advantage quality payments means less funding for the security infrastructure that's supposed to keep this data safe. That doesn't make sense to me. Why would we cut the money that pays for the oversight and protections we desperately need?

Please don't make it easier for this to happen to other families. Keep the strong safeguards in place. Patient safety has to come first.
synth_objective: oppose the proposed reduction of Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments
synth_personal_hook: Last year my mom's insulin pump got hacked when some app I didn't understand shared her medical info with who-knows-where, and we spent three months dealing with identity theft and getting her accounts locked down. I'm terrified if they make it easier to share this stuff around that more people like her are gonna get hurt, especially seniors who don't know what's happening.
synth_core_arguments: Removing security and privacy safeguards in health IT certification will expose vulnerable seniors and patients to data breaches, identity theft, and medical fraud—just like what happened to my mom | Rushing to deregulate without maintaining strong information blocking protections prioritizes corporate profits over patient safety and puts caregivers in an impossible position trying to protect loved ones from tech they don't understand | Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments fund the security infrastructure and oversight that keeps patient data safe—cutting those payments means less money for the protections we desperately need
synth_framing: A caregiver's plea to keep strong guardrails on health data sharing because real families are already suffering real harm when those protections fail.

Comment:
My daughter had an accident on a job site last month and ended up in the ER. After she got out I just wanted to get her records to our regular doctor so we could follow up, but it turned into this whole thing. Three weeks of phone calls, faxing stuff back and forth, people telling me to call somewhere else. It was a mess and honestly it made me mad because we just needed her information moved over.

But now I'm hearing they want to make it easier for all our health stuff to get shared around with apps and companies and I gotta say that really worries me. Two years ago my social security number got stolen when some store got hacked. Two years and I'm still dealing with the fallout from that. So when I hear they want to loosen up the rules on who can see our health information, that scares me. I don't know these app companies. I don't know if they're going to keep my family's information safe or if it's just going to get stolen again.

And look, the Medicare Advantage plan my wife and I have, it gives us extra help with our doctor visits and prescriptions. That matters for us. I hear they're talking about cutting the money that pays for that care coordination stuff. I need to know that my doctor is actually there for me, not that the plan got gutted so some tech company could make a quick buck.

Please keep the rules in place that protect our data. Innovation is fine but not when it means my family's information gets passed around to people I don't trust.
synth_objective: oppose the proposed reduction of Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments
synth_personal_hook: My daughter had to go to the ER last month after a fall on the job site, and I was trying to get her medical records transferred to her regular doctor so we could follow up, but it took three weeks of calling around and faxing stuff back and forth—it was a nightmare. But now I'm hearing they want to make it easier for people to just share all that information with apps and whoever, and that scares me because my social security number and everything got compromised two years ago when some store got hacked, so I don't know who to trust with my health stuff anymore.
synth_core_arguments: Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments fund the care coordination and extra benefits that help people like me actually get care when we need it—cutting those payments means less money for the doctors and nurses who help us | I'm worried about my family's health information being shared around to apps and companies I don't know, especially after my identity got stolen—we need strong rules to keep that data safe, not weaker ones
synth_framing: A working person worried that deregulation will make it easier for his family's sensitive health data to get stolen while cutting the quality care he depends on.

Comment:
My wife had her lab results mixed up with another patient's at the clinic last year. Three weeks. That's how long it took to figure out the mistake, and those three weeks were terrifying for both of us. We didn't know if her medications were right, if her doctors were looking at the wrong numbers, nothing. A nurse caught it before anything bad happened, but I keep thinking what if nobody had noticed?

Now I'm reading about making it easier for health information to move around between different apps and companies, and I get worried all over again. I understand the idea is supposed to help patients, but my concern is that the fewer rules you have about who can access medical data and how they handle it, the easier it becomes for something to go wrong. Or worse, for somebody to steal information.

Here's the thing that really matters though. The Medicare Advantage plans my wife uses have enough staff and oversight because of the quality bonus payments. Those people catching errors, coordinating her care between doctors, making sure nothing falls through the cracks—that's what kept her safe last year. If you cut those payments to speed up deregulation, you're cutting the very safety net that protects people like her.

I'm not against technology or making things easier. I just don't want to trade away the careful oversight and certified safety standards that catch mistakes before they become disasters. Can't we have interoperability without removing the rules that keep data secure and care coordinated?
synth_objective: oppose the proposed reduction of Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments
synth_personal_hook: My wife got her lab results mixed up with somebody else's at the clinic last year, and it took three weeks to straighten out—scared us half to death. I worry that if all her medical stuff gets easier to pass around to different apps and companies, something like that could happen again, only this time it might be somebody stealing her information instead of just a filing mistake.
synth_core_arguments: Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments fund the extra care coordination and safety checks that caught my wife's lab mix-up—removing those payments means fewer staff to catch errors before they become disasters | Pushing health data into more apps and companies without strong certification rules increases the risk of breaches and identity theft, which scares me more than any convenience gain | As a caregiver, I depend on Medicare Advantage plans having the resources to coordinate my wife's care across multiple providers—cutting quality bonuses directly hurts that coordination
synth_framing: A working caregiver worried that deregulation will trade away the safety nets and quality oversight that protect vulnerable patients like his wife for the sake of tech company convenience.

Comment:
My granddaughter had her identity stolen from a data breach last year. It took her six months to fix everything—maxed out credit cards, fraudulent accounts, the whole nightmare. She's twenty-three years old and already dealing with this mess. Now I hear you're talking about loosening up the rules on who can access our medical records and what companies have to do to keep them safe. That scares me.

I don't understand all the computer stuff, but I know what happens when nobody's watching. Companies will cut corners. They always do. You loosen the rules a little bit and suddenly some tech company that promises they'll "self-regulate" and "protect patient data" gets hacked and thousands of people's medical information is out there on the dark web or wherever. Just like my granddaughter. Just like it always happens.

Before you go removing certification requirements or whatever this is about, I need to know who's actually checking that these companies are doing what they say they're doing. Because right now it sounds like you're asking us to trust that tech companies will do the right thing without anybody looking over their shoulder. I've been driving trucks for forty years. I know when somebody's trying to sell me something that's going to break down the minute the warranty's up.

My Medicare Advantage coverage already helps me afford my medications. If you cut payments to make room for more corporate convenience, that's going to hurt people like me. Please don't make patient safety the price of somebody's profits.
synth_objective: oppose the proposed reduction of Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments
synth_personal_hook: My granddaughter got her identity stolen last year from some data breach, and it took her six months to clean up the mess—credit cards maxed out, the whole nine yards. I'm already worried enough about hackers getting into things they shouldn't, and now you're talking about making it easier for more places to grab our medical records. I don't understand all the computer stuff, but I know when something sounds like it's asking for trouble.
synth_core_arguments: Loosening security rules will make it easier for hackers and bad actors to steal medical information, just like what happened to my granddaughter | I don't trust tech companies to protect our private health data if the government stops checking up on them | Medicare Advantage already helps me afford my care—cutting payments will make it worse, not better
synth_framing: Barbara worries that deregulation prioritizes corporate convenience over patient safety, and she's seen firsthand how data breaches destroy lives.

Comment:

Comment:
My daughter was diagnosed with type 1 diabetes about a year ago and managing her care has been a nightmare. I'm an elementary school teacher so I don't have a ton of flexibility, but I'm basically juggling test results and appointment notes from her pediatrician, the endocrinologist across town, and the school nurse. Everything is in different systems and nobody talks to each other so I end up calling people constantly just to ask "did you get this lab result" or "what did the doctor say last week." It's exhausting and honestly I worry I'm going to miss something important because the information is so scattered.

But here's the thing—and maybe I'm old fashioned—I'm terrified about loosening the rules around how companies handle her data. My sister's mental health records somehow got mixed up with her employer and it took her months to straighten out. If we're cutting back on oversight to "reduce burden on developers" or whatever the reasoning is, I'm worried that's just code for making it easier for companies to share data without really protecting it. My daughter's diabetes information is sensitive and I need to know it's actually being kept safe.

I get that the systems need to be able to talk to each other. That would genuinely help families like mine. But the Medicare Advantage plans that help coordinate this care are already cutting corners on quality. If we reduce the incentives for them to maintain secure integrated systems, why would they bother? They'll just cut costs instead. We need rules that make interoperability easier without sacrificing privacy and data protection.
synth_objective: oppose the proposed reduction of Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments
synth_personal_hook: My daughter got diagnosed with type 1 diabetes last year, and I've been trying to keep track of all her test results and doctor visits in like five different places—the pediatrician's office, the endocrinologist, the school nurse. I just want to be able to see everything in one spot without having to call and bug people, but honestly I'm already nervous enough about her information being out there, and I don't want to end up like my sister whose insurance company somehow got her mental health records mixed up with her employer.
synth_core_arguments: Medicare Advantage quality bonus reductions will hurt patients like my daughter who depend on coordinated care across multiple specialists | Cutting quality incentives means providers have less motivation to maintain the integrated systems that help families like mine actually access and organize health information safely | I'm already worried about data privacy—we need strong oversight, not deregulation that makes it easier for my daughter's sensitive diabetes records to end up in the wrong hands
synth_framing: As a teacher and parent managing a child's chronic illness across fragmented providers, I need Medicare Advantage plans to stay incentivized to coordinate care and protect my family's health data—not cut corners to save money.

Comment:
My daughter's medical records were shared with some app by a clinic in Baltimore last year without anyone asking us first. Nobody told us it happened. We found out when I got a bill for services we never used and started seeing charges pop up that made no sense. It took me three months of phone calls—three months—to track down where her information went and try to get it fixed. I'm still dealing with it. We're still getting bills.

And now you want to make it easier for companies to do exactly what happened to my family? You want to remove the safeguards that are supposed to stop this?

I'm a stay-at-home parent. I don't have time to be a detective every time some company decides my daughter's health information is theirs to share. I don't have time to fight billing errors caused by loose regulations. Families like mine are already stretched thin and we shouldn't have to spend months undoing the damage from corporate carelessness.

You keep saying this will help patients access their information. Fine. But patients don't need companies passing our data around to third parties without real oversight. We need stronger protections, not weaker ones. And for seniors on Medicare Advantage plans—they're already getting the short end of the stick with those companies cutting corners. If you reduce quality oversight on who can access their data, it's just going to get worse.

Stop removing the rules that protect us. We need them.
synth_objective: oppose the proposed reduction of Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments
synth_personal_hook: Last year my daughter's information got stolen when a clinic shared her records with some app without asking, and it took me three months and countless phone calls to fix it—I'm still getting bills for stuff we never authorized, and now you want to make it even easier for these companies to pass our data around like it's nothing.
synth_core_arguments: Removing privacy and security safeguards will make it easier for companies to misuse patient data, as happened to my daughter | Stay-at-home parents like me don't have time to fight billing errors and data breaches caused by loose regulations—we need stronger protections, not weaker ones | Medicare Advantage plans already cut corners; reducing quality oversight will only make them worse at protecting vulnerable seniors and their families
synth_framing: A frustrated parent who has personally suffered from inadequate health data protections and fears that deregulation will enable more companies to exploit patient information for profit.

During that time, nobody could tell me exactly who was responsible, whether it was the office staff, the electronic health records vendor, or someone else entirely. Comment:
My mom's diabetes records got mixed up with someone else's at her doctor's office last spring. It took three weeks to fix—three weeks of me calling every single day demanding they straighten it out. During that time, nobody could tell me exactly who was responsible, whether it was the office staff, the electronic health records vendor, or someone else entirely. Eventually we got it fixed, but it was a nightmare.

Now I'm reading about this proposed rule that would strip away certification requirements and information blocking safeguards to make it easier for health data to move between apps and companies. And I have to be honest—I'm terrified. Not because I'm against technology or sharing my health information. I'm terrified because of what happened with my mom, and because this rule seems designed to make those kinds of situations worse, not better.

Here's my core concern: if companies can pass patient data around to third-party apps with fewer rules and oversight, who exactly is responsible when something goes wrong? When data gets lost or mixed up, who do patients call? The app developer? The hospital? Some vendor they've never heard of? The current system isn't perfect, but at least there's a chain of accountability. This deregulation creates a maze where nobody is answerable.

I also want to say something about Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments. Those payments fund the coordination and careful oversight that catches errors like what happened to my mom. Cutting those resources means fewer eyes watching out for data breaches and mix-ups. We need stronger safeguards, not weaker ones.
synth_objective: oppose the proposed reduction of Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments
synth_personal_hook: Last spring my mom's diabetes records got mixed up with someone else's at her doctor's office, and it took three weeks to straighten out—I had to call every day demanding they fix it. Now they want to make it even easier for my data to float around between apps and companies, and honestly, I'm terrified that if something goes wrong this time, there won't be anyone accountable because it'll be some third-party app I never even heard of that's holding my information.
synth_core_arguments: Removing certification criteria and information blocking safeguards will make it easier for my health data to be shared with unknown third-party apps without proper accountability or oversight | When things go wrong—like what happened with my mom's diabetes records—there needs to be a clear entity responsible for fixing it; deregulation creates a maze where nobody is accountable | Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments fund the coordination and oversight that catches these errors; cutting those payments means fewer resources to prevent data mix-ups and breaches
synth_framing: I'm not against technology or sharing my health information—I'm against a system where companies can pass my data around to apps I've never heard of with no one responsible when something breaks.

Comment:
My daughter's insurance changed last year and we had to find a completely new doctor. It took three months—three months!—just to get her medical records from the old office because they said everything had to be mailed by hand. I remember being so frustrated, thinking how nice it would be if she could just share her files electronically with whoever needs them. That part makes sense to me.

But then I saw that news story about the big hospital breach where thousands of people got their information stolen, and honestly it scared me. I started thinking about whose hands our medical data might end up in, and I got real worried.

I understand the idea is to make things easier and cheaper, but I'm concerned that cutting rules to help insurance companies save money is going to come at the cost of families like mine. Those Medicare Advantage quality bonuses fund the extra services and care coordination that actually help my daughter get the care she needs without falling through the cracks. If companies can just strip those away to cut costs, what happens to us?

Before you loosen up regulations to let medical records get shared around more easily, I really think you need strong privacy rules and real oversight in place. I've seen what happens when data gets stolen and it's not worth the risk. Please don't remove safeguards just to help corporations save money. We need both easier access AND real protection for our information. That's not too much to ask.
synth_objective: oppose the proposed reduction of Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments
synth_personal_hook: Last year my daughter had to switch doctors when our insurance changed, and it took three months just to get her medical records transferred because the old office said they had to mail everything by hand. I thought it would be nice if she could just share things electronically with whoever she needs to, but then I heard about that big hospital breach on the news where thousands of people's information got stolen, and I got real worried about whose hands our medical information might end up in.
synth_core_arguments: Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments fund the extra services and care coordination that help patients like my daughter actually get the care they need without falling through the cracks | Cutting these payments will force insurance companies to reduce benefits and doctor networks, making it harder for families to access quality care | We need strong privacy rules and oversight before we make it easier to share medical records—I've seen what happens when data gets stolen, and it's not worth the risk
synth_framing: As a working parent, I'm worried that removing safeguards to help insurance companies save money will leave families like mine with fewer doctors to choose from and less protection for our private health information.

Comment:
Last year my sister spent six months fighting to get her oncology records transferred between two hospitals while undergoing chemotherapy. She literally had to pick up CDs from one place and hand-deliver them to another. It's a story I've heard variations of a hundred times in my work mobilizing communities around healthcare access—and it still infuriates me.

Here's what I understand: health IT developers face real compliance costs. I get that. But I'm concerned this proposed deregulation treats patient safety and developer convenience as a zero-sum game, when they don't have to be.

My worry centers on two things. First, Medicare Advantage plans already use quality bonus metrics tied to care coordination outcomes. If we roll back the standards that actually make data exchange possible—the certification requirements that ensure systems can talk to each other—we're not reducing burden on seniors with chronic conditions. We're shifting it to them. A patient on five medications across three specialists needs seamless data flow. That's not regulatory overreach; that's the baseline for safe care.

Second, on information blocking: we need to clarify what "access" and "use" mean in practical terms, not eliminate the concept entirely. A provider shouldn't be able to weaponize data silos against patients. But the current rules are imperfect. Let's fix them thoughtfully, incrementally. Bring patient advocacy groups to that table—not just industry. We've learned the hard way that when technologists and administrators design healthcare systems without hearing from the people actually using them, we end up with the same nightmare my sister faced.

Reform, yes. Wholesale rollback? That's the wrong direction.

synth_personal_hook: Last year, my sister had to fight for six months to get her oncology records transferred between hospitals—she literally had to pick up CDs from one place and hand-deliver them to another while undergoing chemo. I get that paperwork is a burden for doctors, but watching her go through that made me realize there's got to be a middle ground between protecting people's medical information and actually letting them access it when their life depends on it.

synth_framing: As someone who's mobilized communities around healthcare access, I've learned that cutting corners on data standards doesn't free patients—it traps them in the same bureaucratic nightmare my sister faced.

Comment:
My daughter set me up with a health app about a year ago after I had some heart trouble, and I've got to say it's made a real difference. I can track my blood pressure every morning, see all my numbers in one place, and it gives me peace of mind knowing my doctor can see the same information I'm seeing. That kind of secure system didn't exist when I was younger, and for a veteran like me who's always believed in keeping my personal business private, it's actually helped me feel more in control of my health instead of less.

What worries me though is that these tools cost money to build and keep secure, and I know they wouldn't exist without the Medicare Advantage quality bonuses that help plans pay for this kind of care coordination. I'm just worried that if those bonuses get cut, the plans won't be able to afford maintaining these apps anymore, and I'll loose access to something that's become pretty important to me.

I also don't want to see rules get loosened up just for the sake of loosening them if it means my medical information ends up less protected. I've worked hard my whole life to keep my personal matters private, and I don't want my health data floating around without strong safeguards in place. Before removing certification requirements, I'd ask that you make sure the privacy and security protections stay strong. Let the developers innovate, sure, but not at the expense of veterans like me who are depending on these systems to stay secure and available.
synth_objective: oppose the proposed reduction of Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments
synth_personal_hook: My daughter helped me set up one of those health apps last year so I could track my blood pressure after my heart scare, and it's been real helpful seeing all my numbers in one place. But then I started wondering who else could see that stuff, you know, and it made me nervous thinking about my information floating around out there when I've worked hard my whole life to keep my business and personal matters private.
synth_core_arguments: Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments fund the health technology and care coordination tools that help veterans like me manage chronic conditions safely | Reducing these payments will force plans to cut back on the secure health apps and monitoring systems that give me peace of mind about my data | Deregulation of health IT without strong privacy safeguards puts my medical information at risk, especially after I've worked my whole life to keep my personal matters private
synth_framing: A veteran worried that cutting Medicare Advantage bonuses will strip away the trusted, secure health tools he relies on and expose his hard-earned privacy to unnecessary risk.

Comment:
**Comment of SAFI Clinical Informatics Group LLC to the Office of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology**

The certification burden facing mid-market health IT vendors is not theoretical. When SAFI Clinical Informatics pitched our interoperability solution to a regional hospital network in the Upper Midwest last year, the network's procurement team provided a stark assessment: ONC-ACB certification alone would cost approximately $400,000—a figure that our larger competitors absorb as routine operating expense but that represents an existential barrier for vendors of our scale. This comment addresses a critical gap in the deregulatory proposal: burden reduction for developers is meaningless without corresponding stability in provider reimbursement.

The proposed rule correctly identifies that inflated certification pathways entrench incumbent vendors and suppress genuine market competition. However, this deregulatory relief will fail to achieve its pro-competition intent if concurrent Medicare Advantage payment reductions—driven by separate policy decisions—shrink provider budgets for health IT investment. Smaller vendors face a structural disadvantage: our per-unit compliance costs are substantially higher than those of dominant market players. We depend on sustained provider purchasing power to justify the capital investment required to navigate certification. When providers face simultaneous pressure to cut operational spending due to lower MA reimbursement, they default to proven solutions from vendors they already know.

The RFI asks which certification criteria should be revised to reduce burden while maintaining interoperability and patient safety. We recommend that ONC prioritize streamlining the attestation and documentation requirements for criteria addressing standards conformance and data exchange. Many current pathways require redundant third-party testing that could be consolidated without compromising safety. However, we urge ASTP and ONC to explicitly coordinate with CMS to ensure that MA payment policy does not undermine the competitive effects this rule is designed to achieve. Deregulation paired with payment stability is pro-competition. Deregulation paired with provider margin compression is simply consolidation by another mechanism.
synth_objective: oppose the proposed reduction of Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments
synth_personal_hook: When we pitched our interoperability solution to a regional hospital network last year, they told us the certification pathway alone would cost us $400,000—money we simply don't have as a mid-sized vendor—while the major players absorb that like pocket change and lock in these contracts for another five years.
synth_core_arguments: Medicare Advantage quality bonus payment reductions will disproportionately harm mid-sized health IT vendors who lack the economies of scale to absorb certification and compliance costs that larger competitors easily manage | Deregulatory relief must be paired with Medicare Advantage payment stability—otherwise, reduced reimbursement will shrink provider budgets for health IT investment, negating any compliance burden reduction for smaller vendors | The certification pathway cost barrier ($400K+) already locks smaller vendors out of regional contracts; further payment cuts to providers will only entrench incumbent vendors and reduce genuine market competition
synth_framing: A mid-market health IT director arguing that Medicare Advantage payment cuts will undermine the deregulatory rule's pro-competition intent by starving smaller vendors of the provider investment needed to offset their higher per-unit compliance costs.

Comment:
My granddaughter's pediatrician's office got hacked last year and my Social Security number was stolen. I'm 67 years old and on Medicare and it took me months to fix the damage—months of calling around, worrying every time mail showed up. I still get nervous. Now I'm hearing that the government wants to make it easier for health information to be shared with all these apps and companies, and I have to speak up because this terrifies me.

I don't understand why we would loosen the rules when hackers are already getting through. The people in charge keep saying companies will police themselves and that innovation is good for patients. It'll be from the corners they cut—and I'm worried those corners include the people and systems that are supposed to keep my data safe.

My granddaughter deserves to grow up in a world where her health information isn't treated like it's worthless. Please don't remove the safeguards. Make them stronger.

Comment:
Re: RIN 0945-AA17 – Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions

Waymark, Inc., a Wisconsin-based health IT vendor, submits this comment in support of streamlined certification frameworks, while raising critical concerns about the interaction between deregulation and concurrent Medicare Advantage payment policy.

Last year, a Milwaukee-based EHR vendor with genuinely innovative population health capabilities ceased development and dissolved its certification pipeline. The company's product was technically sound, but cumulative compliance costs—audit fees, standards documentation, vendor lock-in provisions—had become untenable without institutional venture backing. This was not market failure; it was regulatory burden concentrating market power.

We support ONC's deregulatory intent. However, we urge caution about unintended consequences when deregulation proceeds in isolation from Medicare payment policy.

**The Consolidation Paradox**

If certification burden is reduced while simultaneously Medicare Advantage quality bonuses are compressed, smaller and regional vendors face a perverse choice: invest the savings from lighter certification into product robustness and true interoperability, or absorb margin pressure from payers and reinvest in compliance cost reduction. Market dynamics will push toward the latter. Vendors operating on 15–20 percent margins—typical for mid-market players—cannot voluntarily sacrifice competitive positioning to maintain interoperability standards that larger incumbents have already built into their cost structure.

The result will not be innovation; it will be accelerated consolidation, precisely as occurred in the Milwaukee market. Large national vendors will dominate, smaller innovators will exit, and payers will have fewer negotiating partners.

**Recommendation**

To address the RFI question on reducing developer burden while maintaining interoperability: Deregulate certification procedurally—streamline submission, reduce documentation overhead, shorten timelines—but do not weaken substantive interoperability and patient safety criteria. Simultaneously, preserve MA quality payment levels sufficient to support healthy margins for mid-market vendors. This paired approach allows smart deregulation without sacrificing the competitive ecosystem that drives real innovation.

Regional health IT ecosystems cannot survive on regulatory relief alone when reimbursement incentives work against them. Policy coordination, not sequential cuts, will unlock genuine competition.

Respectfully submitted,

Thomas Thomas
Director of Government Relations
Waymark, Inc.
Wisconsin
synth_objective: oppose the proposed reduction of Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments
synth_personal_hook: When our company's smaller competitor in Milwaukee had to abandon their EHR certification pipeline last year due to mounting compliance costs, I watched a genuinely innovative team dissolve—not because their product wasn't sound, but because the certification apparatus had become prohibitively expensive for anyone without venture backing. That experience crystallized for me how the current framework inadvertently consolidates market power among the few players who can absorb these costs, while locking out the kind of scrappy regional vendors who used to drive real competition in this space.
synth_core_arguments: Medicare Advantage quality bonus reductions will disproportionately harm smaller health IT vendors and regional innovators who lack the scale to absorb margin compression, further consolidating market power among large national players | Cutting MA quality payments while simultaneously deregulating certification standards creates a perverse incentive: vendors will optimize for compliance cost reduction rather than interoperability robustness, ultimately undermining the patient safety and data integrity that certification exists to protect | Regional health IT ecosystems depend on sustainable margins for mid-market vendors; bonus payment reductions will accelerate the same consolidation dynamic that eliminated genuine competition in our market
synth_framing: A government relations professional concerned that simultaneous deregulation and payment cuts will paradoxically entrench monopolistic incumbents while eliminating the scrappy regional competitors who drive real innovation.

Comment:
Last spring I needed my granddaughter's birth records from a different hospital system, and it took six weeks and three phone calls to get them transferred. Six weeks! For a newborn's basic records. So I understand why people say we need faster information sharing. But here's the problem: I can't separate that frustration from what happened to me five years ago.

My identity was stolen through a data breach at a bank. Five years, and I'm still dealing with it. Still getting bills I never opened accounts for. Still freezing my credit every few months. The thought that my diabetes medication list, my psychiatric history, all of that could be sitting on servers at app companies and their contractors with minimal oversight—it keeps me up at night.

I'm on a fixed income as a retired teacher. I depend on my Medicare Advantage plan for affordable coverage and the care coordination that actually works for me. I read the news. I know what's being discussed. And my worry is that deregulation is moving faster than the privacy safeguards that need to come with it.

I'm not against apps sharing data. I'm against doing it backwards. Don't remove the guardrails first and add privacy protections later. That's how people like me get hurt again.

Before you loosen certification requirements for health IT companies, strengthen the rules around what they can actually do with my information. Make sure access is controlled. Make sure 'use' has real limits. I want faster records. I need privacy more.
synth_objective: oppose the proposed reduction of Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments
synth_personal_hook: Last spring, my granddaughter's pediatrician needed records from when she was born at a different hospital system, and it took six weeks and three phone calls to get them transferred—it was maddening. But when I read that apps might soon pull my medical information directly, I got nervous, because my identity was stolen five years ago through a data breach at a bank, and I'm still cleaning that mess up. I want my records to move faster, but not if it means every app company and their contractors have access to my diabetes medication list and psychiatric history.
synth_core_arguments: Medicare Advantage quality bonus reductions will hurt seniors like me who depend on those plans for affordable coverage and access to coordinated care | Deregulation of health IT without stronger privacy safeguards will expose vulnerable patients to the same kind of data breaches that have already devastated my life | Faster record-sharing sounds good in theory, but not if it means my sensitive medical information gets passed to app companies with minimal oversight
synth_framing: As a retired teacher on a fixed income who has lived through identity theft, I need Medicare Advantage to stay strong AND I need real privacy protections—not a choice between the two.

Comment:
My wife Janet had thyroid surgery last year and I spent three months calling different doctors offices just trying to get copies of her test results. Three months. They kept saying it would take weeks and it was incredibly frustrating when we needed that information to help coordinate her care. Her sister is a nurse and she could of helped us understand what was going on if we could just access the records, but instead we were stuck waiting around.

I know you're trying to make it easier for people to get their health information faster and share it with apps and other providers. That part makes sense to me. But here's what keeps me up at night—if you loosen up the rules around how all this data moves around to make things go faster, how do we know Janet's sensitive medical information stays safe? I hear about hacking all the time and her information is just floating around on computers somewhere. That scares me.

The Medicare Advantage plans actually invested in the technology that eventually helped us get better access to her records. That kind of investment matters. If those bonus payments get cut, I worry the plans won't have the resources to build systems that are both fast and secure. We need both things, not one or the other.

I'm not against making things work better. But whatever you do, please don't sacrifice the privacy and security safeguards that protect patients like my wife just to speed things up. We need trustworthy systems, not just fast ones.

synth_personal_hook: My wife Janet had her thyroid surgery last year, and I spent three months calling around trying to get copies of her test results so I could help her track what was going on—the doctors kept saying it would take weeks. If we could've just pulled that information up ourselves and shared it with her sister who's a nurse, things would've been a lot easier on us. But honestly, I'm nervous about all her personal stuff floating around on computers somewhere it could get hacked, you know?

synth_framing: A caregiver worried that deregulation prioritizing tech speed over security safeguards could harm patients like his wife who depend on coordinated, trustworthy care.

Comment:
**Comment on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity**

Skyward IT Solutions appreciates the Administration's intent to reduce unnecessary regulatory burden on health IT innovation. However, the sequencing of proposed reforms risks achieving the opposite result—consolidation rather than competition—unless certification barriers are dismantled before Medicare Advantage reimbursement incentives are curtailed.

Last year, a promising vendor approached Skyward about integrating their EHR module with our platform. The technical architecture was sound; the clinical value proposition was genuine. Their compliance officer quoted $180,000 in ONC-ACB certification costs to achieve Full Certification. That startup walked away. We did not lose an integration opportunity—we watched an innovative solution die on a spreadsheet because compliance investment could not be justified against the vendor's available capital. This is not anecdotal. Across the health IT sector, certification burden has become a de facto market-entry tax that only incumbents can absorb.

The proposed rule appears to acknowledge this problem. Yet cutting Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments—a material revenue stream for smaller vendors—without first removing redundant ONC certification criteria and lowering ACB barriers creates a perverse outcome: vendors will face simultaneously higher compliance costs and lower reimbursement justification for undertaking those costs. The result will not be a level playing field. It will be accelerated consolidation.

Skyward does not oppose deregulation. We support it. But deregulation must be sequenced rationally. ONC should prioritize identifying and removing certification criteria that duplicate USCDI attestations or impose compliance burden disproportionate to interoperability and patient safety gains. Lowering ACB examination fees and streamlining the certification pathway for modular functionality should precede—not accompany—reimbursement reduction.

Once certification barriers fall, market entry becomes rational even at lower Medicare Advantage payments. Until then, cutting reimbursement simply accelerates consolidation among vendors who have already absorbed the compliance sunk cost.
synth_objective: oppose the proposed reduction of Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments
synth_personal_hook: Last year, we had a promising startup approach us about integrating their EHR module with our platform—the technical fit was perfect—but they walked away after their compliance officer quoted them $180K just to get ONC certification. I watched a genuinely innovative solution die on the spreadsheet because the barrier to entry had become prohibitive, and frankly, it made me wonder whether the vendors who can absorb those costs are the ones who should be setting the standards.
synth_core_arguments: Medicare Advantage quality bonus reductions will disproportionately harm smaller health IT vendors and startups who lack the capital to absorb compliance costs, further consolidating the market among large vendors | Certification burden is already a market-entry killer for innovative solutions; reducing Medicare Advantage incentives compounds this by eliminating a key revenue stream that could offset compliance investment | The proposed deregulation should prioritize removing redundant certification criteria and lowering ONC-ACB barriers before cutting reimbursement—the two moves should be sequenced, not conflated
synth_framing: A pragmatic technologist who has watched innovation die at the compliance spreadsheet and fears that cutting Medicare Advantage payments without first dismantling certification barriers will simply accelerate consolidation rather than unlock competition.

Comment:
My daughter's identity got stolen in 2021 when the hospital system where she was treated got hacked. It took her six months and hundreds of dollars out of her own pocket to straighten out the mess—credit fraud, accounts opened in her name, phone calls from debt collectors for charges she never made. I was working as a nurse at the time and I watched her go through it. It was devastating.

Now I'm reading about loosening up the rules around how patient information gets passed around, and I have to speak up. I spent thirty years in nursing and I can tell you from experience that every time you hand off a patient's information to another system or company, theres a chance something goes wrong. Theres a window where bad actors can get in. Hospitals and tech companies say they want to make things easier and more efficient, but what they really want is to cut costs. They'll reduce those security certifications and loosen the information blocking rules because it saves them money, not because it helps patients like my daughter.

The quality bonuses that Medicare Advantage plans get—those are what actually fund the security measures that protect us. Cut those payments and you cut the money for safeguards. Were not talking about some abstract problem here. Were talking about real families losing sleep over stolen identities.

Please keep the protections in place. Don't let them weaken the rules just to save companies a few dollars.
synth_objective: oppose the proposed reduction of Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments
synth_personal_hook: My daughter had her identity stolen two years ago when that hospital's computer system got hacked, and it took her six months and hundreds of dollars to fix it all. Now they want to make it even easier for our medical records to get passed around to who knows where—I worked in nursing long enough to know that every time you hand off a patient's information, something can go wrong.
synth_core_arguments: Reducing certification criteria and privacy safeguards will make it easier for hackers and bad actors to access patient data, putting families at risk like my daughter was | Hospitals and tech companies are pushing this deregulation to save money, not to help patients—they'll cut corners on security and we'll pay the price | Medicare Advantage plans use quality bonuses to fund better security and patient protections; cutting those payments means less money for the safeguards we need
synth_framing: A retired nurse who knows firsthand how data breaches destroy lives is warning that loosening health IT rules will sacrifice patient privacy for corporate convenience.

Comment:
My daughter spent three weeks last year fighting with my cardiologist's office just to get copies of my test results. I get it—patients need access to their own records and it shouldn't be that hard. But now I'm reading that you might be loosening the rules around how our medical information gets shared with all these apps and third parties, and honestly it terrifies me.

If my medical records start floating around to different apps without strong security oversight, what's stopping someone from doing the same thing to me? I worked in nursing for thirty years and I saw how careful we had to be with patient information. That mattered. I'm worried that if you remove the safeguards that require these companies to prove they can actually protect our data, we're just handing our most sensitive information to whoever wants it.

I also don't understand why cutting the quality bonus payments for Medicare Advantage plans is part of this. Those bonuses help pay for the care coordination and security measures that are supposed to watch over who's accessing our records. Who's going to be watching if that money disappears?

I'm not against innovation. I just need to know my information is actually safe.

My name is Rebecca Martin and I'm a retired teacher living in California on a fixed pension. I wanted to share something that's been on my mind about healthcare access because I think it matters a lot.

Last year my daughter was diagnosed with Type 1 diabetes and we had to switch pediatricians. It took six weeks just to get her medical records transferred because they insisted on mailing everything instead of sending it electronically. Six weeks. When a kid needs continuous care that's really sc
Comment:
My name is Rebecca Martin and I'm a retired teacher living in California on a fixed pension. I wanted to share something that's been on my mind about healthcare access because I think it matters a lot.

Last year my daughter was diagnosed with Type 1 diabetes and we had to switch pediatricians. It took six weeks just to get her medical records transferred because they insisted on mailing everything instead of sending it electronically. Six weeks. When a kid needs continuous care that's really scary for a parent. So I understand wanting to make it easier for information to move around quickly between doctors and apps.

But here's what worries me. I'm on Medicare Advantage and honestly those extra benefits—the ones funded by quality bonuses—they're what keep my healthcare affordable on my teacher's pension. My plan keeps my copays down and helps me afford the medications I need. If those quality bonuses get cut, I'm terrified the plans will just drop benefits or raise premiums. I can't absorb higher costs. A lot of seniors like me can't.

I'm not an expert in all this technology stuff, but I do know that the quality standards tied to those bonuses actually work. They make sure doctors stay in the network and that plans take care of us.

I'm all for making things work better and reducing red tape where it doesn't matter. But please don't let that mean cutting the safeguards that keep seniors like me from losing our healthcare. We need both—innovation and protection.

Thank you for listening.
synth_objective: oppose the proposed reduction of Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments
synth_personal_hook: My daughter got diagnosed with Type 1 diabetes last year, and when we were trying to switch pediatricians, it took six weeks to get her records transferred because the old office said they had to mail everything instead of sending it electronically—it was so frustrating when she needed continuous care. Now I'm worried that if we make it too easy for apps to grab all that medical info, someone could steal her information and use it, especially since kids' identities are worth more on the black market, or so I've read.
synth_core_arguments: Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments fund the extra benefits and care coordination that help seniors like me afford medications and specialist visits | Cutting these bonuses will force plans to reduce benefits or raise premiums, which hurts fixed-income retirees who can't absorb those costs | The quality metrics tied to these bonuses actually work—they incentivize plans to keep doctors in network and maintain good service
synth_framing: As a retired teacher on a fixed income, I depend on Medicare Advantage's extra benefits, and cutting quality bonuses will make my healthcare less affordable and harder to access.

We thought we had something that could actually compete. Then we started looking into what it would take to get certified, and we hit a wall: $140,000 just to get through the initial certification process. That's not a barrier to entry. That's a wall.

Here's what frustrates me about this deregulatory proposal. On
Comment:
Our team at InContext spent eighteen months building a hospital scheduling system that our three pilot sites confirmed could cut their administrative overhead by 30%. We were excited. We thought we had something that could actually compete. Then we started looking into what it would take to get certified, and we hit a wall: $140,000 just to get through the initial certification process. That's not a barrier to entry. That's a wall.

Here's what frustrates me about this deregulatory proposal. On paper, it sounds great—reduce burden, cut compliance costs, let innovators compete. But that's not what happens in practice. When you lower certification costs without addressing the fixed-cost structure of compliance, you're not helping startups. You're helping Epic and Cerner write slightly smaller checks while we're still locked out entirely.

The big vendors can absorb a $140K certification cost. They do it across fifty products. For a startup like ours, that's runway. That's payroll. That's survival.

The RFI asks which criteria should be removed to reduce burden. Fair question. But I'd ask a harder one: How does removing standards help us when hospitals will just default to Epic anyway because certification still costs more than our entire development budget? Deregulation without equity doesn't level the playing field. It just speeds up consolidation.

Medicare Advantage payment reductions will make this worse. Smaller vendors and startups won't just be priced out—we'll be unnecessary. The incumbents will own the whole market.

Real innovation means small companies can actually enter it.
synth_objective: oppose the proposed reduction of Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments
synth_personal_hook: My startup partner and I spent eighteen months developing a scheduling system that our three pilot hospitals said could cut their administrative overhead by 30%, but when we finally looked into what certification would actually cost us, we're talking $140,000 just to get through the door—money we don't have and that the big players like Epic just write off as pocket change.
synth_core_arguments: Medicare Advantage quality bonus payment reductions will disproportionately harm smaller health IT vendors and startups trying to compete with entrenched players, because compliance costs remain fixed regardless of company size | Reducing certification burden sounds good in theory, but without clear standards, hospitals will default to Epic and Cerner anyway—so the real winners are the incumbents, not innovators like us | The $140K certification barrier is already killing our market entry; further deregulation without cost relief for small vendors just means the big players get cheaper compliance while we're still locked out
synth_framing: A scrappy health IT entrepreneur frustrated that deregulation rhetoric benefits large corporations while smaller innovators like his startup remain priced out of the market.

During that time I was terrified. What if a doctor had prescribed something based on the wrong chart? What if her insulin dosing had gotten confused with someone else's? I worked in nursing long enough to know how fast things can go wrong when patient information isn't handled carefully.

Comment:
My daughter's medical records got completely mixed up with another patient's at Kaiser Permanente last year. It took us three months—three months—to get it straightened out. During that time I was terrified. What if a doctor had prescribed something based on the wrong chart? What if her insulin dosing had gotten confused with someone else's? I worked in nursing long enough to know how fast things can go wrong when patient information isn't handled carefully.

Now I'm hearing about this push to loosen up regulations on health IT companies and these third-party apps so they can access patient data easier. I understand the idea sounds good in theory but I'm honestly scared. These tech companies care about profit and growth, not about my daughter's safety. I've seen firsthand how even big established healthcare systems like Kaiser can mess up data management. Why would we make it easier for companies I've never even heard of to access sensitive medical information when we can't even trust the hospitals to keep records straight?

And I keep hearing about cutting Medicare Advantage bonus payments. Those bonuses actually matter—they're what pushes plans to invest in the careful oversight that prevents medical errors and data mistakes. If we're removing safeguards AND cutting the money that funds quality control, we're basically asking for another disaster.

Innovation is fine but not at the expense of patient safety. My daughter has diabetes and deserves to know her information is protected, not floating around to the highest bidder.
synth_objective: oppose the proposed reduction of Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments
synth_personal_hook: My daughter's diabetes records got mixed up with someone else's at Kaiser last year, and it took three months to straighten out — I can't even imagine what would happen if her information was floating around on some app I never heard of, especially with all these hackers out there nowadays.
synth_core_arguments: Removing privacy and security safeguards in the name of 'innovation' puts vulnerable patients like my daughter at serious risk of data breaches and identity theft | Medicare Advantage plans currently invest in quality care partly because of bonus incentives — cutting those payments means less money for the careful oversight that prevents medical errors and data mix-ups | I've seen firsthand how bad data management can be; loosening regulations on third-party apps and health IT developers will only make it worse, not better
synth_framing: A retired nurse who trusts regulated healthcare systems but fears what happens when profit-driven tech companies get easier access to patient data without strong oversight.

Comment:
Last year when I had knee surgery, I spent three weeks going back and forth between my orthopedist's office and my physical therapist trying to get my imaging results. They kept saying the scans were "in the system" but nobody could actually pull them up and send them over. It was frustrating and it slowed down my recovery. So I get it—we need better ways to share medical records.

But here's the thing: my sister had her identity stolen in 2019 through a healthcare data breach. It took her almost a year to clean up the mess—fraudulent accounts, bad credit, the whole nightmare. That happened when there were already safeguards in place. I'm worried that if we strip away certification requirements and security standards just to make things easier and faster, we're going to create more opportunities for that kind of thing to happen to people like me.

I'm not against sharing my data more easily. I just need to know it's actually secure when it moves around. And honestly, the reason those systems that do work well—the ones that balance access with security—exist at all is because Medicare Advantage plans have funding from quality bonus payments to invest in that infrastructure. They use those dollars to build secure platforms and pay for the tech expertise to keep them that way.

If we're going to push for better interoperability, we can't do it by cutting the funding that actually makes secure systems possible. That's not a trade-off we should be making.
synth_objective: oppose the proposed reduction of Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments
synth_personal_hook: Last year when I had my knee surgery, I spent weeks trying to get my imaging results so my physical therapist could see them—kept getting transferred between offices and told things were "in the system." If my records were easier to access and share, it would've saved me a lot of frustration. But I also remember my sister got her identity stolen a few years back through a healthcare data breach, and it took her months to sort out. I just hope we can figure out a way to make these records easier to get without making it easier for the wrong people to steal them.
synth_core_arguments: Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments fund the care coordination and technology investments that actually help patients like me access and understand our medical records | Cutting these payments will force plans to choose between maintaining interoperability infrastructure and keeping premiums affordable for working people | We need better data sharing, but not at the cost of the security safeguards that protect us from identity theft—and not by gutting the funding that makes those safeguards possible
synth_framing: Margaret speaks as someone who's experienced both the frustration of fragmented medical records and the real harm of a data breach, arguing that quality bonus payments are what actually fund the secure, accessible systems patients need.

Abstract:
The certification regime governing health information technology has created a two-tiered market that systematically disadvantages smaller vendors, and the proposed reduction in Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments will accelerate this consolidation rather than remedy it.

OneSelf Technology spent $180,000 on compliance documentation alone to certify our patient portal last year—a figure that consumed nearly 40% of our annual IT operations budget. Three of our eight engineers devoted themse
Comment:
The certification regime governing health information technology has created a two-tiered market that systematically disadvantages smaller vendors, and the proposed reduction in Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments will accelerate this consolidation rather than remedy it.

OneSelf Technology spent $180,000 on compliance documentation alone to certify our patient portal last year—a figure that consumed nearly 40% of our annual IT operations budget. Three of our eight engineers devoted themselves full-time to translating our already-secure codebase into ONC-mandated formats for twelve weeks. We are not a regulatory resistance case study; we are a company that believes in interoperability and patient safety. But when I compare our certification burden to the economics at a $2 billion vendor, where compliance costs vanish into overhead and are amortized across thousands of customers, the asymmetry is not a gap—it is a chasm.

The Medicare Advantage bonus structure, whatever its original intent, has functioned as the primary financial justification for smaller providers to adopt certified interoperable systems. Those bonuses offset the real cost differential that smaller vendors face in certification. Remove that incentive, and providers face a choice: invest in compliance-certified solutions from vendors with enough scale to absorb the compliance burden, or postpone interoperability investments altogether. Either way, smaller vendors like ours lose the revenue case that justifies our certification spend.

I do not believe the solution is to weaken certification standards. Patient safety and genuine interoperability demand rigor. Rather, the certification criteria themselves should be revised to reduce redundant documentation requirements and streamline the technical format burden that does not correlate with actual security or interoperability outcomes. The ONC should examine which of its current certification modules generate compliance cost disproportionate to their clinical or data-exchange value.

Cutting MA bonuses while leaving the certification burden intact will not unleash prosperity for health IT innovation. It will consolidate the market further toward vendors large enough to absorb both burdens. If the Administration is serious about competition, it must address certification burden *before* removing the revenue streams that make smaller-vendor participation viable.
synth_objective: oppose the proposed reduction of Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments
synth_personal_hook: When our startup was trying to get our patient portal certified last year, we spent $180,000 on compliance documentation alone—money we could've invested in actually improving the security infrastructure—while watching a major vendor roll out nearly identical functionality with their compliance costs buried in their $2B annual budget. My team of eight engineers spent three months just translating our code documentation into the required format, and I watched two of them leave for bigger companies because we couldn't compete on salary while hemorrhaging money on certification.
synth_core_arguments: Medicare Advantage quality bonus reductions will disproportionately harm smaller health IT vendors who lack the economies of scale to absorb compliance costs, further consolidating the market toward mega-vendors | Cutting MA bonus payments removes the financial incentive for providers to invest in interoperability infrastructure and certified health IT systems, ultimately degrading data exchange capabilities | The certification burden is already crushing innovation at the startup level—reducing MA bonuses compounds this by eliminating the revenue stream that justifies compliance investment
synth_framing: A health IT director argues that Medicare Advantage bonus cuts will entrench big-vendor dominance by starving smaller competitors of the revenue needed to afford certification compliance, while simultaneously removing provider incentives to adopt interoperable systems.

It took her eight months—eight months—to straighten out the mess with the credit card companies and the banks. She was stressed out of her mind. I watched her go through it and it was awful. Now I'm hearing that regulators want to make it easier for my health information to be shared around to all these different apps and programs, and frankly I'm terrified. I don't want that happening to me.

I s
Comment:
My daughter's identity got stolen two years ago when her doctor's office got hacked. Two years ago. It took her eight months—eight months—to straighten out the mess with the credit card companies and the banks. She was stressed out of her mind. I watched her go through it and it was awful. Now I'm hearing that regulators want to make it easier for my health information to be shared around to all these different apps and programs, and frankly I'm terrified. I don't want that happening to me.

I spent thirty years as a nurse. I know how important it is to keep patient information secure. But I also know what happens when that security falls apart. People's lives get turned upside down.

What concerns me most is that you're talking about removing safeguards without making sure we still have strong security standards in place. You can't just let companies do whatever they want because it's more convenient for them. That's backwards. And I've heard that the quality bonus payments that Medicare Advantage plans get right now—those payments help fund security measures. If you cut those, where's the money going to come from to protect our data?

I'm begging you not to rush into this. Don't make it easier for hackers to target medical records. Keep the protections in place. My daughter learned the hard way what happens when you don't, and I'm not willing to find out myself.
synth_objective: oppose the proposed reduction of Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments
synth_personal_hook: My daughter got her identity stolen two years ago when that medical office she goes to got hacked, and it took her eight months to straighten everything out with the credit companies. Now I hear they want to make it easier for my information to be shared around to all these apps and programs, and I'm terrified the same thing is going to happen to me—I've got enough on my plate without having to deal with that nightmare again.
synth_core_arguments: Removing privacy and security safeguards will put patients like me at risk of identity theft and data breaches, just like what happened to my daughter | Medicare Advantage plans currently use quality bonus payments to fund security measures—cutting those payments means less money for protecting patient data | Rushing to deregulate health IT without strong security standards is reckless when hackers are already targeting medical records
synth_framing: A retired nurse who's seen firsthand how data breaches destroy lives, warning that deregulation prioritizes corporate convenience over patient safety.
