Third Space, Inc. appreciates the Administration's commitment to reducing regulatory burden in health IT and welcomes the opportunity to comment on mechanisms for meaningful certification reform. However, we must flag a structural sequencing problem in the current approach that risks undermining its own innovation objectives.

Eighteen months ago, Third Space spent approximately $400,000 and eight
Comment:
RE: Request for Information on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability Deregulatory Actions

Third Space, Inc. appreciates the Administration's commitment to reducing regulatory burden in health IT and welcomes the opportunity to comment on mechanisms for meaningful certification reform. However, we must flag a structural sequencing problem in the current approach that risks undermining its own innovation objectives.

Eighteen months ago, Third Space spent approximately $400,000 and eight months navigating ONC certification requirements for our interoperability platform. That experience taught us the certification process is indeed cumbersome. But it also revealed something less obvious: the economic model that justified that investment in the first place. Medicare Advantage plans, facing quality bonus calculations tied to interoperability metrics under 42 CFR § 422.560, had genuine financial incentive to adopt our tools. That demand signal made the compliance burden rational, if punishing.

The current proposal addresses burden reduction through selective deregulation of certification criteria—a reasonable direction in isolation. Yet it occurs simultaneously with reductions in Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments. We've watched this movie before. Removing certification friction is valuable only if the market economics that reward compliance remain intact. Strip both the regulatory stick *and* the financial carrot, and vendors face a different calculus entirely.

For smaller innovators without in-house regulatory counsel or series funding to absorb compliance costs, the math becomes untenable. Larger competitors with established relationships, legacy infrastructure, and economies of scale will absorb certification burden more easily and can operate profitably even with diminished MA incentives. Smaller firms get stranded—sunk costs unrecovered, market entry abandoned. Consolidation accelerates.

To the RFI question on reducing certification burden while maintaining quality assurance: meaningful burden reduction requires identifying which certification criteria actually correlate with interoperability failures versus which simply impose compliance cost. That distinction matters. But it matters only if the underlying payment structures continue signaling to health plans that interoperability investment is worth making.

We support regulatory modernization. We urge, however, that any revision to certification requirements be coupled with preservation of Medicare Advantage incentive structures that create demand for interoperable systems. Deregulation sequenced without attention to market dynamics will consolidate incumbent advantage—the opposite of stated intent.
synth_objective: oppose the proposed reduction of Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments
synth_personal_hook: When we tried to bring our interoperability platform to market eighteen months ago, our compliance team spent eight months and roughly $400,000 just navigating certification requirements—costs that our Series A funding barely absorbed. I watched a larger competitor with in-house regulatory counsel move through the same process in four months at a fraction of the expense, and it crystallized something I'd been sensing: the current framework doesn't just protect patients, it calcifies market advantage for whoever can afford to play the long game.
synth_core_arguments: Medicare Advantage quality bonus reductions will disproportionately harm smaller health IT vendors who lack the economies of scale to absorb compliance costs, further entrenching incumbent market power | Cutting quality bonus payments removes the financial incentive for plans to invest in interoperability infrastructure and data standards adoption, paradoxically undermining the deregulation agenda's stated innovation goals | The proposed changes conflate burden reduction with safety—but selective deregulation of certification criteria without corresponding liability frameworks will shift compliance risk onto vendors without reducing actual market entry barriers
synth_framing: A pragmatic industry insider who supports regulatory modernization in principle but warns that poorly-sequenced deregulation of Medicare Advantage incentives will consolidate power among large incumbents while leaving smaller innovators exposed.

Comment:
My wife's diabetes records got mixed up with somebody else's at our clinic last year. It took three months—three months of phone calls, back and forth, trying to get her actual files separated from some other patient's information. The staff kept apologizing but couldn't figure out how it happened. Eventually we got it sorted, but I kept thinking: if they can't keep straight files in one office with maybe a dozen people, how in the world are they going to protect our information when it's bouncing around between apps and companies we've never even heard of?

Now I'm reading that you want to make it easier for all these applications to grab our health data. Remove the rules. Let the market figure it out. I get that innovation is supposed to be good, but I'm a librarian—I work with information systems every day—and I know the difference between theoretical benefits and what actually works.

The quality bonus payments that fund Medicare Advantage programs? Those pay for the care coordinators and staff who actually caught my wife's error. The oversight. The people who care about getting it right. If you cut those bonuses to push interoperability, you're taking money away from the people watching the system. Before we let more companies access our data, we need to see that the basic protections already in place can actually work. My family's experience proves they can't. Don't trade real patient safety for the promise of innovation. We need better oversight, not less.
synth_objective: oppose the proposed reduction of Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments
synth_personal_hook: Last year my wife's diabetes records got mixed up with somebody else's at our clinic, and it took three months of phone calls to straighten out. Now they want to make it easier for all these apps to grab our information, and I'm telling you, if they can't even keep straight files in one office, how are they going to protect it when it's flying around to who knows where?
synth_core_arguments: Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments fund the care coordination and care management programs that caught my wife's medical records error—removing those payments will reduce the staff and oversight that prevent dangerous mistakes | Before we let more apps and companies access our health data, we need to see that the basic systems already in place can actually protect it—my family's experience proves they can't | Cutting quality bonuses to push interoperability sounds like trading real patient safety for theoretical innovation benefits
synth_framing: A frustrated patient and library worker who sees data security failures in real time and fears that deregulation will make healthcare information less safe, not more innovative.

**Comment on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity**

Freedom Home Health Care Inc. operates across Kansas with approximately 120 employees, and we have direct experience with how certification burden and reimbursement structure interact to prevent market competition. Last month, we terminated a promising partnership with a regional health IT vendor—not because their solution was inferior, but because their entire first-year operating
Comment:
**Comment on Health Data, Technology, and Interoperability: ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions To Unleash Prosperity**

Freedom Home Health Care Inc. operates across Kansas with approximately 120 employees, and we have direct experience with how certification burden and reimbursement structure interact to prevent market competition. Last month, we terminated a promising partnership with a regional health IT vendor—not because their solution was inferior, but because their entire first-year operating budget was consumed by ONC certification and compliance infrastructure costs. This vendor demonstrated interoperability capabilities that our internal analysis suggested could reduce our documentation errors by approximately 15%, yet the financial barrier to market entry made them non-viable before we could conduct a pilot program.

The proposed deregulation appropriately targets certification burden as an obstacle to innovation and competition. However, addressing certification costs in isolation will not achieve the stated objective of enabling smaller vendors to compete. The critical gap in this proposal is the simultaneous reduction of Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments. For health systems like ours, these bonuses provide the financial predictability necessary to absorb implementation risk when piloting solutions from newer market entrants.

Even if certification costs decrease substantially, our organization cannot justify implementing an unproven system from a startup vendor without revenue stability to cushion potential pilot failures. The vendor we lost did not fail because certification was impossible—they failed because their startup capital was exhausted before they could prove their value to a risk-averse customer base. Reducing certification burden while eliminating the Medicare Advantage revenue floor that enables health systems to take measured risks on smaller vendors will paradoxically entrench the market concentration the deregulation intends to solve.

We strongly support the certification burden reduction framework. We urge the Agency to reconsider the Medicare Advantage quality bonus cuts as currently structured, or to implement a transition period that preserves sufficient revenue predictability for health systems to pilot solutions from smaller vendors during the compliance cost reduction period. Without this coordination, deregulation will achieve lower barriers to entry but not actual market entry.
synth_objective: oppose the proposed reduction of Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments
synth_personal_hook: Last month, we lost a promising local vendor partnership because their certification costs had already consumed their first-year operating budget—they couldn't afford the compliance infrastructure we needed, so we defaulted to the national player we've been stuck with for eight years. What frustrated me most was knowing that this smaller company had genuinely innovative interoperability solutions that could have reduced our documentation errors by an estimated 15%, but the barrier to entry made them non-viable before we could even pilot their system.
synth_core_arguments: Reducing Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments will further entrench large vendors by eliminating the compliance cost relief that smaller innovators need to compete | The current certification burden already killed a viable local partnership that could have improved our interoperability and reduced documentation errors by 15%—deregulation must include Medicare Advantage incentive protection to actually enable market entry for smaller vendors | Without Medicare Advantage quality bonuses as a revenue floor, health systems like ours cannot afford to pilot or adopt smaller vendors' solutions, even when they're technically superior, because we lack the financial cushion to absorb implementation risk
synth_framing: A health IT director arguing that Medicare Advantage quality bonus cuts will paradoxically worsen the very market concentration problem that deregulation is supposed to solve, because smaller vendors need both lower certification costs AND stable revenue incentives to survive the pilot phase.

Abstract: My daughter Sarah was diagnosed with Type 1 diabetes about fourteen months ago. For the first few months after her diagnosis, it was chaos trying to get a complete picture of her care. Her pediatrician had some labs, her endocrinologist had others, and her regular doctor had different records entirely. Nobody could see the full story of her A1C levels or what was really going on. It took me weeks of phone calls and faxing papers around just to piece things together, and during that time Sarah wa
Comment:
My daughter Sarah was diagnosed with Type 1 diabetes about fourteen months ago. For the first few months after her diagnosis, it was chaos trying to get a complete picture of her care. Her pediatrician had some labs, her endocrinologist had others, and her regular doctor had different records entirely. Nobody could see the full story of her A1C levels or what was really going on. It took me weeks of phone calls and faxing papers around just to piece things together, and during that time Sarah wasn't getting the coordinated care she needed.

Then something shifted. Her Medicare Advantage plan started pushing all three doctors to actually talk to each other and share information electronically. Suddenly her A1C results were available to everyone. Her insulin adjustments made more sense because the endocrinologist could see what the pediatrician was doing. The system finally worked the way it should have from the start.

I understand you're trying to reduce burden on technology companies and make innovation easier. I get that. But I'm worried about what happens if we loosen up the privacy rules just to make things cheaper or faster. My daughter's data is sensitive. It's her medical history, her diagnosis, her treatment plan. If we remove safeguards so companies can cut corners and save money, who's protecting kids like Sarah?

What I'm asking is don't make us choose between better coordination and real privacy protection. The Medicare Advantage bonuses actually incentivized the kind of care sharing that helped my daughter. Before cutting those, consider whether the tradeoff is worth it. Please keep strong security rules in place alongside any new data sharing requirements. That's not deregulation that helps patients—that's deregulation that hurts them.
synth_objective: oppose the proposed reduction of Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments
synth_personal_hook: When my daughter was diagnosed with Type 1 diabetes last year, we had to piece together her medical records from three different doctors just to get a clear picture of her A1C levels and test results, and it frustrated me that the information was scattered everywhere—but now I'm worried that if we can share all that sensitive stuff more easily, someone could steal her data and use it against her somehow.
synth_core_arguments: Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments drive better care coordination and information sharing that helped my daughter get faster diagnoses | Cutting these bonuses will hurt the integrated care systems that finally made her medical records accessible across her three doctors | We need strong privacy rules alongside any data sharing—not a choice between one or the other
synth_framing: A retired teacher worried that deregulation will sacrifice the patient safety safeguards that made modern coordinated care possible for his diabetic daughter.

Abstract:
**Comment Submitted on Behalf of Customer Value Partners, LLC**

Donald Campbell, Vice President of Regulatory Affairs
Customer Value Partners, LLC
Illinois

---

The stated objective of removing certification burdens to unlock innovation is sound in principle, yet the proposed approach risks achieving the opposite result. Our firm has spent the past eighteen months working with a promising EHR module developer—a three-person team with genuine technical innovation in interoperability workflows.
Comment:
**Comment Submitted on Behalf of Customer Value Partners, LLC**

Donald Campbell, Vice President of Regulatory Affairs
Customer Value Partners, LLC
Illinois

---

The stated objective of removing certification burdens to unlock innovation is sound in principle, yet the proposed approach risks achieving the opposite result. Our firm has spent the past eighteen months working with a promising EHR module developer—a three-person team with genuine technical innovation in interoperability workflows. Last month, they withdrew from integration with our platform. The reason was unambiguous: ONC-ACB certification and USCDI compliance would cost $215,000 to $280,000, depending on the scope and testing complexity. For a startup operating on venture capital with an eighteen-month runway, that figure was insurmountable. Our larger competitors absorbed similar costs as a business-unit rounding error and moved forward.

This is not a complaint about patient safety standards. It is an observation about market structure. Reducing Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments under 42 CFR § 422.560 without simultaneously addressing the certification cost barrier will not spur competition—it will accelerate consolidation. Margin compression hits smaller vendors hardest, and when certification remains expensive, only incumbents with scale can afford both lower reimbursement and the fixed costs of compliance. The startup we lost will not return; it will be acquired by a larger player, or it will exit health IT entirely. Neither outcome serves patients or innovation.

The rule cannot credibly claim to reduce burden while leaving the certification infrastructure intact. We respectfully submit that the RFI should prioritize a targeted reduction in redundant standards requirements—particularly duplication across FHIR-based certification pathways and legacy USCDI attestation cycles. A phased approach to removing overlapping testing criteria, combined with streamlined ONC-ACB review workflows, would materially lower the cost floor for entry without compromising interoperability validation.

Payment reform and certification reform must move together. One without the other simply repositions the competitive disadvantage, leaving truly innovative smaller vendors unable to participate in either space.
synth_objective: oppose the proposed reduction of Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments
synth_personal_hook: Last quarter, we had a promising startup reach out about integrating their EHR module with our platform, but they withdrew after learning the certification pathway would cost them north of $200K—money they simply didn't have. Meanwhile, I watched our larger competitors absorb those costs as a rounding error and consolidate market share. It crystallized for me how the current structure isn't protecting patients; it's just entrenching incumbents while talented innovators get priced out of the game.
synth_core_arguments: Medicare Advantage quality bonus payment reductions will disproportionately harm smaller health IT innovators who lack the scale to absorb margin compression, further entrenching incumbent vendors and reducing genuine market competition. | The current certification and compliance burden already creates a $200K+ barrier to entry for promising startups; reducing MA payments without simultaneously streamlining certification criteria will accelerate consolidation rather than foster the innovation the rule purports to enable. | True interoperability and patient safety are better served by lowering certification costs and removing redundant standards requirements than by cutting reimbursement incentives—the rule should address both simultaneously or risk creating a two-tier market where only large vendors can afford compliance.
synth_framing: A regulatory affairs executive frustrated that deregulation rhetoric ignores the real cost structure that locks out innovators, arguing that payment cuts without certification reform will worsen, not solve, the consolidation problem.

Abstract:
My daughter's allergist's office was hacked last year. We didn't know until my wife's credit monitoring service flagged fraud attempts tied to her social security number. Then came three months of calling companies, freezing accounts, and living in this fog of worry that we'd miss something. I'm 66 now, and I still think about it more than I'd like to admit.

So when I hear about loosening up health IT rules in the name of innovation and competition, I hear something different: I hear corners be
Comment:
My daughter's allergist's office was hacked last year. We didn't know until my wife's credit monitoring service flagged fraud attempts tied to her social security number. Then came three months of calling companies, freezing accounts, and living in this fog of worry that we'd miss something. I'm 66 now, and I still think about it more than I'd like to admit.

So when I hear about loosening up health IT rules in the name of innovation and competition, I hear something different: I hear corners being cut. And I'm worried sick about Medicare Advantage beneficiaries—people like me and my wife—becoming the guinea pigs for that experiment.

Here's what worries me most. If you reduce quality bonus payments to Medicare Advantage plans, where do you think that money is going to come from? It's not coming from shareholder profits. It's coming from security infrastructure. Data protection costs money. Hiring good IT staff costs money. Audits and breach response plans cost money. Once you squeeze those lines in the budget, seniors get hurt.

I understand the appeal of making it easier for different systems to talk to each other. That sounds reasonable. But certification standards exist because patient data isn't like a pair of shoes—it's our medical histories, our social security numbers, our whole identities. You can't just rush to market with a "move fast and break things" approach when what gets broken is someone's financial life.

Don't weaken Medicare Advantage standards. Please. My family has lived through this nightmare once. I don't want anyone else to.
synth_objective: oppose the proposed reduction of Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments
synth_personal_hook: Last year my daughter's allergist's office got hacked and our family's medical records ended up somewhere on the dark web—we only found out because a credit monitoring service flagged suspicious activity tied to my wife's social security number. Now every time I think about making my own health information easier to access or share, I remember spending three months dealing with fraud alerts and calling different companies to lock down our accounts, and I wonder if convenience is really worth that headache happening again.
synth_core_arguments: Medicare Advantage quality bonus reductions will force plans to cut corners on security infrastructure and data protection, putting vulnerable seniors at greater risk of identity theft and medical fraud | Deregulation of health IT certification and information blocking rules prioritizes corporate convenience over patient safety—I've lived through the nightmare of a data breach and won't accept that risk being pushed onto Medicare beneficiaries
synth_framing: A retired teacher who experienced healthcare data breach fallout warns that weakening Medicare Advantage quality standards in the name of 'innovation' will expose seniors to the same identity theft and privacy violations that devastated his family.

Comment:
My sister learned the hard way what happens when you trust the wrong health app. Last year she downloaded something that promised to help manage her medications better. Seemed simple enough. Within weeks her bank account was emptied and her identity was compromised. It took her months to sort everything out and she's still dealing with the fallout.

That's why I'm really concerned about loosening the safeguards on health IT certification. I get that companies want to move faster and cut costs, but patient safety can't be the thing we sacrifice to make that happen. My medical information isn't just data to shuffle around—it's the most sensitive stuff about me and my family.

I'm already worried that when I go in for my next checkup my doctor is going to push some app on me because it saves the hospital money, not because it's actually secure or because I understand what I'm agreeing to. I don't want to be pressured into using technology that could put me at the same kind of risk my sister went through. There's got to be a way to reduce burden on developers without stripping away the security and privacy protections that actually keep patients safe.

Also, I know a lot of seniors on Medicare Advantage plans and those quality bonus payments matter to them. Cutting those to save money in the system just shifts the cost onto people who can least afford it. We shouldn't be making health care cheaper by making it riskier or worse.
synth_objective: oppose the proposed reduction of Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments
synth_personal_hook: Last year my sister got her records hacked after some app promised to track her medications, and the thieves cleaned out her bank account before anyone caught it. Now whenever I go to my doctor appointments, I'm worried they're gonna push me to use some app I don't understand, and I don't want the same thing happening to me or my family.
synth_core_arguments: Removing security and privacy safeguards in health IT certification will put patients like me and my family at serious risk of data breaches and identity theft | I don't want hospitals and doctors pushing me to use apps and technology I don't fully understand just because it's cheaper for them—my medical information is too important to gamble with | Medicare Advantage plans already help seniors afford care; cutting quality bonus payments will make them worse, not better
synth_framing: As a working person who's seen firsthand how data breaches destroy lives, I'm worried that loosening health IT rules will sacrifice patient safety for corporate convenience.

Comment:
**Medplum's Comments on ASTP/ONC Deregulatory Actions: The Incomplete Picture of Market Competition**

The stated objectives of this rulemaking—reducing certification burden and enabling smaller vendors to compete—are sound. Yet after fifteen years of watching health IT consolidation accelerate despite multiple waves of interoperability mandates, I must respectfully observe that deregulation alone cannot solve the structural problem we face.

At Medplum, we welcomed our fourth integration partner last year. Our compliance officer spent six weeks documenting FHIR conformance and certification criteria—work that diverted resources from the actual interoperability investment our clients needed. The vendors we approached who lacked similar documentation infrastructure simply could not compete on equal footing. They were not excluded by regulation; they were excluded by the cumulative compliance overhead that larger incumbents had already absorbed years prior. This is the invisible moat the rule assumes deregulation will dissolve. It will not, absent accompanying payment certainty.

**The Policy Collision**

The rule's proposed reduction of certification criteria addresses the symptom but not the disease. Simultaneously reducing Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments—currently under separate consideration—creates perverse incentives. Smaller vendors and integrated delivery networks depend disproportionately on MA contracts to fund compliance infrastructure. If those payments decline while certification requirements remain substantive, vendors will optimize for regulatory compliance avoidance rather than genuine interoperability investment. The market will consolidate further, not less.

**A Structural Constraint**

The burden-reduction objective requires paired commitment: either maintain Medicare Advantage payment stability while reducing certification load, or reduce both simultaneously in a coordinated fashion. Otherwise, the rule inadvertently subsidizes consolidation among players already positioned to absorb compliance costs.

If the agency intends to revise certification criteria, we respectfully recommend focusing on criteria that duplicate information blocking compliance under 42 CFR § 171.302, rather than criteria that address patient safety or genuine interoperability gaps. This reduces burden without hollowing out the standards infrastructure smaller vendors depend on to prove competitive parity.

Deregulation is necessary but insufficient. Payment certainty is the prerequisite for smaller vendors to compete fairly on interoperability merit.
synth_objective: oppose the proposed reduction of Medicare Advantage quality bonus payments
synth_personal_hook: When we onboarded our fourth integration partner last year, I watched our compliance officer spend six weeks just documenting interoperability standards—work that could have gone toward actually building the connections our clients needed. The smaller vendors we wanted to work with simply couldn't afford the same documentation overhead, so we kept cycling back to the same three established players who had already absorbed those costs years ago. It's created this invisible moat that's frustrating precisely because it's not malicious, just structural.
synth_core_arguments: Medicare Advantage quality bonus reductions will disproportionately harm smaller health IT vendors and integrated delivery networks that lack the economies of scale to absorb compliance costs, further consolidating the market around established players | Deregulatory relief on certification and interoperability standards should be paired with Medicare Advantage payment stability—removing one without the other creates perverse incentives where vendors optimize for regulatory compliance avoidance rather than genuine interoperability investment | The structural barriers to market entry (documentation overhead, certification costs) that currently favor incumbents will only worsen if Medicare Advantage payments decline, since smaller vendors depend on MA contracts to fund compliance infrastructure
synth_framing: A pragmatic industry insider acknowledging that deregulation alone won't solve market concentration—payment certainty is the prerequisite for smaller vendors to compete fairly on interoperability.
